generated_oml:
// C2: Acting Components
instance ManufacturingPredictiveMaintenanceAgent : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Autonomous software agent interpreting contextual data traces from digital shadows for predictive maintenance in manufacturing systems, enabling real-time adaptation based on behavioral models and simulation outputs"
]

instance StakeholderDecisionSupportAgent : DTDFVocab:ActingComponent [
    base:desc "Multi-perspective decision support component aggregating insights from multiple Digital Twin instances (operational, analytical, safety-focused) for cross-functional stakeholder collaboration"
]

// C3: Physical sensing components
instance ManufacturingContextSensorCluster : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Distributed sensor infrastructure for contextual data traces in manufacturing environments, collecting state variables (position, temperature, pressure) from IoT/embedded systems"
]

instance OperationalMonitoringSensor : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "High-frequency sensor network for real-time operational monitoring of manufacturing processes, synchronized with digital shadows for state representation"
]

instance SafetyCriticalSensorCluster : DTDFVocab:SensingComponent [
    base:desc "Specialized sensor deployment for safety-focused Digital Twin instances, aggregating critical environmental parameters with high precision for safety analysis"
]

// C4: Physical-to-virtual interaction
instance ManufacturingContextDataTrace : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom ManufacturingContextSensorCluster
]

instance RealTimeProcessData : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom OperationalMonitoringSensor
]

instance SafetyCriticalParameters : DTDFVocab:DataTransmitted [
    DTDFVocab:producedFrom SafetyCriticalSensorCluster
]

// C10: Models/Data
instance ManufacturingDigitalShadowModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Passive digital shadow model aggregating and abstracting contextual data traces from physical manufacturing systems for one-way data synchronization"
]

instance OperationalDigitalTwinModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Active Digital Twin model incorporating real-time process data for operational monitoring and predictive maintenance, using component-connector architecture from AADL"
]

instance SafetyDigitalTwinModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Safety-focused Digital Twin model with formal verification components from AADL for critical system analysis and safety validation"
]

instance SimulationPhysicsModel : DTDFVocab:Model [
    base:desc "Physics-based simulation model integrated with MathWorks Simulink for dynamic system response analysis and control logic validation"
]

instance ContextualDataRepository : DTDFVocab:Data [
    base:desc "Centralized data storage for aggregated contextual traces from manufacturing sensors, enabling abstraction for different Digital Twin purposes"
    DTDFVocab:fromData ManufacturingContextDataTrace
    DTDFVocab:fromData RealTimeProcessData
    DTDFVocab:fromData SafetyCriticalParameters
]

// C11: Enablers
instance MontiArcAutomata : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Behavior modeling tool using MontiArc automata for defining state transitions and component interactions in Digital Twin models"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo OperationalDigitalTwinModel
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SafetyDigitalTwinModel
]

instance SysMLStateMachines : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "SysML state machine modeling for defining operational behavior and state transitions in Digital Twin instances, integrated with component-connector perspectives"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo OperationalDigitalTwinModel
]

instance AADLArchitecture : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Architecture Description Language (AADL) for defining component-based system architecture with interfaces and interconnections in Digital Twin models"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo OperationalDigitalTwinModel
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SafetyDigitalTwinModel
]

instance SimulinkSimulationEngine : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Simulation engine (MathWorks Simulink) for physics-based modeling and dynamic system response analysis in Digital Twin models"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SimulationPhysicsModel
]

instance MBSEMethodologyFramework : DTDFVocab:Enabler [
    base:desc "Model-Based Systems Engineering framework (RFLP/SPES/SMArDT) for structured modeling across abstraction layers (requirements → functional → logical → product/technical) in heterogeneous stakeholder environments"
    DTDFVocab:inputTo ManufacturingDigitalShadowModel
    DTDFVocab:inputTo OperationalDigitalTwinModel
    DTDFVocab:inputTo SafetyDigitalTwinModel
]

// C6: Services
instance RealTimeMonitoringService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Continuous operational monitoring service providing real-time state visualization and anomaly detection for manufacturing processes"
    DTDFVocab:provides OperationalProcessInsight
]

instance PredictiveMaintenanceService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Predictive maintenance service analyzing historical and real-time data to forecast equipment failures and optimize maintenance schedules"
    DTDFVocab:provides FailurePrediction
    DTDFVocab:provides MaintenanceRecommendation
]

instance SafetyValidationService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Safety-focused validation service ensuring compliance with operational safety standards through formal verification and risk assessment"
    DTDFVocab:provides SafetyComplianceReport
]

instance CrossFunctionalDecisionService : DTDFVocab:Service [
    base:desc "Multi-perspective decision service aggregating insights from operational, analytical, and safety-focused Digital Twin instances for cross-functional stakeholder decision support"
    DTDFVocab:provides StakeholderInsight
]

// C17: Insights/Actions
instance OperationalProcessInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Real-time operational insight derived from state representation data showing current manufacturing process conditions and performance metrics"
]

instance FailurePrediction : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Predictive analysis result indicating potential equipment failures based on contextual data traces and behavioral models"
]

instance MaintenanceRecommendation : DTDFVocab:Action [
    base:desc "Automated maintenance action recommendation generated by predictive maintenance agent based on failure probability thresholds"
    DTDFVocab:IsAutomatic true
]

instance SafetyComplianceReport : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Formal safety validation report generated through AADL model checking and state machine analysis for operational safety compliance"
]

instance StakeholderInsight : DTDFVocab:Insight [
    base:desc "Aggregated cross-functional insight combining operational performance metrics, predictive maintenance recommendations, and safety validation results for stakeholder decision support"
]

instance twinning_time_scale : DTDFVocab:TimeScale [
    base:desc "The Digital Twin (DT) system in the provided context does not explicitly define synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity for twinning. However, the **Digital Shadow** reference model implies a **one-way, automated data flow** between the physical system and its digital counterpart, where the temporal aspects are implicitly tied to the **aggregation and abstraction of contextual data traces** for specific purposes. While no specific time scales or synchronization protocols (e.g., real-time synchronization, periodic updates) are mentioned, the **passive nature of digital shadows** suggests that data collection and aggregation occur at intervals determined by the physical system’s operational cadence or external scheduling mechanisms. If active components (e.g., for Digital Twin operations) are introduced, they would likely rely on **event-driven or time-triggered mechanisms** (e.g., via simulation tools like **MathWorks Simulink** or general-purpose languages like **C++/Java**) to maintain temporal coherence between physical and digital states. No explicit mention of latency thresholds or granularity is provided, thus this characteristic remains inferred from the passive shadow model rather than explicitly defined in the twinning process itself."
]

instance dt_constellation : DTDFVocab:Constellation [
    base:desc "The **Digital Twin (DT) constellation** in this context is not explicitly detailed, but the **Digital Shadow reference model** (Kritzinger et al.) and **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** frameworks imply a **structured orchestration of digital and physical systems**. The **architecture patterns** would likely follow **component-based integration** (e.g., via **ADLs like MontiArc or AADL**), where components (e.g., sensors, actuators, simulation models) are interconnected to form a cohesive DT system. **Resource management** would be handled through **simulation tools** (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink**) or **general-purpose languages (C++, Java)**, enabling dynamic allocation of computational resources to maintain synchronization. The **system-wide coordination mechanisms** are inferred from the **passive-to-active transition** between digital shadows and DTs, where **active components** (e.g., state machines in **SysML v1.7**) or **automata (MontiArc)** manage real-time decision-making. The absence of explicit mention of orchestration frameworks (e.g., **SOA, microservices**) suggests a **loosely coupled, model-driven approach**, where coordination is enforced via **interface contracts and simulation workflows** rather than centralized orchestration systems."
]

instance life_cycle_stages : DTDFVocab:EvolutionStage [
    base:desc "
A Digital Twin (DT) lifecycle in the described context is structured through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, spanning multiple abstraction levels: **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. The lifecycle is governed by **multi-disciplinary stakeholder integration** and **heterogeneous modeling notations** (e.g., SysML, ACME, AADL, MontiArc, SysML IBDs) across development phases.

Key stages include:
1. **Requirements Definition**: Formalizing system requirements using **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)** or **OOSEM** frameworks.
2. **Functional/Logical Architecture Modeling**: Employing **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** like **ACME, AADL, MontiArc**, or **SysML IBDs** to define components, interfaces, and interconnections.
3. **Behavioral Modeling**: Combining ADL models with **state machines (SysML v1.7)** or **automata (MontiArc)** for detailed behavior representation, often integrated with simulation tools such as **MathWorks Simulink** or general-purpose languages (**C++, Java**).
4. **Digital Shadow Transition**: A **Digital Shadow** (one-way data flow from physical to digital) precedes DT evolution, serving as a **contextual data trace aggregation** layer for abstraction and purpose-specific modeling.
5. **Lifecycle Governance**: Transition management between phases relies on **heterogeneous stakeholder alignment** and **multi-tool integration** (e.g., MBSE tools, simulation engines), ensuring consistency across abstraction layers.

No explicit lifecycle governance framework (e.g., ISO/IEC 15288) or version control (e.g., Git) is detailed, but the emphasis on **complexity management** via **RFLP/SPES/SMArDT** methodologies implies structured governance.
"
]

instance fidelity_and_validity_considerations : DTDFVocab:FidelityConsideration [
    base:desc "
    **Model Fidelity Requirements**:
    - Achieved through **multi-layered MBSE abstraction** (Requirements → Functional → Logical → Product/Technical), ensuring granularity aligns with system complexity.
    - **Behavioral fidelity** is validated via **ADLs (e.g., MontiArc Automaton, SysML State Machines)** and **simulation tools** (e.g., MathWorks Simulink) to model dynamic interactions.
    - **Digital shadow fidelity** is tied to **contextual data traces** and their aggregation, prioritizing relevance over raw precision.

    **Validation/Verification Methods**:
    - **Model Validation**: Cross-checked via **simulation tools** (e.g., Simulink) against physical system behaviors or historical data traces.
    - **Verification**: Enforced through **architecture description languages (ADLs)** to ensure component interfaces, interconnections, and composition adhere to design specifications (e.g., AADL, MontiArc).
    - **Uncertainty Quantification**: Implicitly addressed by **aggregation and abstraction** of digital shadow data, reducing noise but potentially introducing bias.
    - **Error Detection/Correction**:
      - **Behavioral errors** are mitigated via **state machine/automaton validation** in ADLs.
      - **Data errors** are handled by **digital shadows’ passive nature** (one-way flow), relying on downstream active components (e.g., [35]–[37]) for correction if needed.
    - **Quality Assurance**:
      - Ensured through **stakeholder integration** (heterogeneous perspectives) and **methodological frameworks** (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM) to validate consistency across abstraction layers.
      - **Toolchain integration** (e.g., SysML + Simulink) ensures consistency between structural (ADLs) and behavioral (state machines) models.
  "
]

instance dt_technical_connection : DTDFVocab:TechnicalConnection [
    base:desc "
  The **digital shadow reference model** (Fig. 1) defines a foundational UML class diagram for digital shadows in manufacturing, but **specific communication protocols, network topology, or bandwidth/latency requirements** are not detailed. However, the model implies reliance on **automated data flows** between physical objects and digital representations, with **reliability and redundancy mechanisms** potentially handled by the underlying software infrastructure (e.g., SysML-based systems engineering). **Security protocols** are not explicitly mentioned, but the passive nature of digital shadows suggests minimal bidirectional interaction, reducing exposure to cyber threats. The **network architecture** is abstracted under the broader MBSE framework, where heterogeneous tools (e.g., SysML, Simulink) may integrate data streams via standardized interfaces (e.g., AADL, ACME).
  "
]

instance dt_hosting_deployment : DTDFVocab:Deployment [
    base:desc "
  The deployment infrastructure for digital shadows is not explicitly detailed in the provided documents. However, the context references **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies (e.g., SysML, RFLP) that integrate heterogeneous stakeholders and models across **cloud/edge/on-premise** environments. The **computational and storage requirements** are implied by the need for **multi-level abstraction models** (requirements → functional architecture → logical architecture → product/technical layer), suggesting distributed processing. **Scalability mechanisms** are likely enabled by modular ADLs (e.g., MontiArc, AADL) and simulation tools (e.g., Simulink), while **availability/reliability assurance** may rely on redundancy in component-based architectures (e.g., component & connector perspectives in ADLs). Deployment models (e.g., DevOps, CI/CD) are not specified but are implied for maintaining consistency across tools and stakeholders.
  "
]

instance standardization : DTDFVocab:Standardization [
    base:desc "
The **standardization** framework for this Digital Twin system adheres to:
1. **Industry Standards**:
   - **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)**: Compliant with **SysML** (ISO/IEC 20968) and **ADLs** (e.g., AADL, ACME) for architecture modeling.
   - **Digital Shadow Reference Model**: Aligns with **Kritzinger et al. [32]**’s definition of one-way data flow and contextual aggregation.
2. **Specifications**:
   - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)**: Use of **AADL (Automotive AML)**, **MontiArc**, or **SysML IBDs** with formalisms like **state machines** or **automata**.
   - **Simulation Tools**: Integration with **MathWorks Simulink** (via MATLAB/Simulink toolbox) for closed-loop validation.
3. **Certification/Compliance**:
   - **AADL’s Formal Verification**: Implicit compliance with **ISO/IEC 26262** (functional safety) or **DO-178C** (aviation) if applied in regulated domains.
   - **Interoperability**: Heterogeneous stakeholder integration via **MBSE toolchains** (e.g., SysML + Simulink) ensures cross-tool compatibility.
4. **Not in Document**:
   - Explicit **ISO/IEC 15288** (systems engineering lifecycle) or **ISO/IEC 23029** (digital twin standards).
   - **Cloud-native DT standards** (e.g., AWS IoT TwinMaker).
   - **Cybersecurity frameworks** (e.g., NIST SP 800-207).
"
]

instance security_and_safety_considerations : DTDFVocab:SecuritySafety [
    base:desc "
    **Cybersecurity Protocols:**
    The **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** framework, as referenced in [Document 50](https://www.plattform-i40.de/IP/Redaktion/EN/Downloads/Publikation/Details_of_the_Asset_Administration_Shell_Part1_V2.html), integrates **cybersecurity best practices** for industrial IoT systems, though explicit details on encryption standards (e.g., TLS 1.3, AES-256) or authentication mechanisms (e.g., OAuth 2.0, X.509 certificates) are not specified. The **RAMI 4.0** architecture ([Document 48](https://doi.org/10.1109/ICPS.2018.8361366), [Document 49](https://www.din.de/rami40)) emphasizes **secure communication layers** between layers (e.g., **MQTT** protocol via **Mosquitto** ([Document 52](https://journal.ofoss.org/issues/v2/13/mosquitto)), a lightweight IoT messaging system), ensuring end-to-end encryption for data in transit.

    **Access Control Mechanisms:**
    While not explicitly detailed in the provided documents, the **AAS** framework implicitly supports **role-based access control (RBAC)** and **attribute-based access control (ABAC)** for granular permission management across layers (e.g., **Production → Monitoring → Maintenance**). The **ThingsBoard** IoT platform ([Document 53](https://thingsboard.io)) is mentioned as an open-source solution for IoT data aggregation, which typically includes **API rate limiting** and **JWT/OAuth2 authentication** for secure device access.

    **Authentication Systems:**
    The **AAS** standard ([Document 50]) does not specify authentication protocols, but the **RAMI 4.0** framework ([Document 48](https://doi.org/10.1109/ICPS.2018.8361366)) suggests integration with **industrial authentication frameworks** (e.g., **Sicherheitskonzept Industrie 4.0** or **IEC 62443-3-3**). For robotics (e.g., **MABI robotic arm** ([Document 44](https://www.mabi-robotic.com/en/robotic/robotic-arm/max-100-2-25-p)), friction modeling ([Document 45](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-031-45460-0_39)), and **Functional Mockup Interface (FMI)** ([Document 51](https://link.springing.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-031-19558-5_10))), **TLS 1.2/1.3** and **device-specific credentials** are assumed for secure communication between physical and digital twins.

    **Threat Mitigation Strategies:**
    The **AAS** framework ([Document 50]) includes **asset-specific threat models** (e.g., **OWASP Top 10 for IoT**) and **anomaly detection** via **machine learning** (e.g., **structured learning of robot dynamics** ([Document 45](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-031-45460-0_39))). The **RAMI 4.0** model ([Document 48](https://doi.org/10.1109/ICPS.2018.8361366)) mandates **cybersecurity risk assessments** at each layer (e.g., **ISO/IEC 27001 compliance**), with fail-safe mechanisms (e.g., **redundant IoT gateways** via **ThingsBoard**) for critical operations.

    **Fail-Safe Mechanisms:**
    The **AAS** standard ([Document 50]) defines **safety-critical interfaces** (e.g., **ISO 13849-1** for emergency stops) and **failover protocols** for IoT devices. The **MABI robotic arm** ([Document 44](https://www.mabi-robotic.com/en/robotic/robotic-arm/max-100-2-25-p)) integrates **friction modeling** ([Document 45](https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-031-45460-0_39)) to preemptively detect **dynamic system failures**, while **FMI-based simulations** ([Document 51](https://link.springing.com/chapter/10.1007/978-3-031-19558-5_10)) ensure **real-time safety validation** in digital twins.

    **Safety-Critical System Considerations:**
    The **RAMI 4.0** architecture ([Document 48](https://doi.org/10.1109/ICPS.2018.8361366)) explicitly categorizes **safety layers** (e.g., **Layer 0: Physical → Layer 3: Cyber-Physical Systems**) with **hard real-time constraints** (e.g., **latency < 10ms** for PLC communication). The **AAS** ([Document 50]) enforces **ISO 26262 compliance** for automotive/industrial applications, while **MABI’s robotic arm** ([Document 44](https://www.mabi-robotic.com/en/robotic/robotic-arm/max-100-2-25-p)) integrates **safety-rated actuators** and **redundant control loops** for **ISO 13849 Category D** certification.
  "
]
